data.title: OMAH: Invest Like Buffett And Get A Double-Digit Yield On The Side
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) announced on Thursday that it would be buying Occidental Petroleum's (OXY) OxyChem unit for nearly $10 billion. Berkshire already owns a substantial position in Occidental, but this acquisition presents an opportunity to put some of that massive cash pile to work and expand his exposure to a company and sector that he's already quite familiar with.

If you're reading this, you're probably also very aware that the ETF industry tends to capitalize on every trend happening in the markets. So it shouldn't be surprising that there's an ETF out there that mimics Warren Buffett's investing style and a substantial portion of Berkshire's top holdings.

But it comes with a twist.

OMAH: Buffett Plus Yield

There's not an ETF that purely mimics the Berkshire Hathaway portfolio (you could just buy Berkshire stock if you wanted that), but there is one that ties in the Buffett philosophy with the current popularity of high yield derivative income strategies.

The VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF (NYSEARCA:OMAH) is an actively managed fund that involves two components:

  • an equity sleeve linked to the Solactive VistaShares Berkshire Select Index
  • an options income sleeve through which the fund aims to achieve an annual income target of 15%, distributed monthly.

The index, as the fund's website notes, is NOT designed to be a clone of Berkshire's entire business. Instead, it will target the 20 "highest valuation" securities from the Berkshire portfolio. To round out OMAH's portfolio, it invests approximately 10% of its holdings into Berkshire Hathaway's stock.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.95%
  • AUM: $581 million (as of 10/2/25)
  • Target Distribution Yield: 15% (monthly pay)
  • Avg. Daily Volume (past 30 days): $14.7 million (as of 10/2/25)
  • Avg. Spread: 0.05% (as of 10/2/25)

OMAH's expense ratio is OK, but not terribly out of range for funds that use an actively managed option overlay strategy. It doesn't use a static strategy either, where it's simply writing a monthly call on an index. It's dynamic and ever-changing (something we'll get into in a moment).

Its nearly $600 million asset base is impressive, given that it only launched earlier this year. Trading nearly $15 million worth of shares daily has helped create a relatively tight spread, making it more than liquid enough to trade regularly.

The fund is actively managed in a couple of different ways. First is the use of the covered call strategy on top of the core equity sleeve. Second, the fund maintains some flexibility in how it allocates to equities.

Because the fund is managed to achieve the 15% distribution yield, it may tilt the equity holdings to help accomplish that. Low volatility stock, which could be a significant piece of Berkshire's portfolio, may not, in the index's current construction, generate the necessary premiums to fund the yield. In that case, it may over-allocate to higher volatility stocks to produce higher premiums.

In most cases, however, it sticks pretty closely to the Berkshire portfolio.

Understanding OMAH's Covered Call Strategy

The key to understanding OMAH's overall risk/return profile is to understand how the management team is layering on the covered calls.

OMAH uses a dynamic strategy, which is to say that it can adjust the percentage of the portfolio that is being overlaid based on current conditions and volatility levels. In general, the fund is writing out-of-the-money options on individual holdings within the fund.

As is the case with all covered call strategies, it alters the risk/return profile when compared to a pure long position in a stock. In short, it caps upside capital appreciation potential in exchange for the high yield.

Generally speaking, maximum profit potential is capped at the strike price of the option contract. Once the share price moves above the option price, the call would, in theory, get exercised, and the seller would have to deliver shares at the strike price.

The covered call seller would be fully exposed to share price downside but would receive the option premium regardless of the movement of the share price. In essence, it provides at least a partial offset to share price downside risk.

That's why covered call strategies generally work best in sideways trending or gently rising markets. The ideal outcome would be to capture the option premium without experiencing any share price loss. Ultimately, it comes down to the goal of the investor. Many choose covered call strategies for the steady income stream and treat share price movements as only a secondary consideration.

This can make OMAH an ideal fit for income investors looking for high yield but also looking to cap volatility.

Is The 15% Target Yield Sustainable?

From a structural standpoint, yes. All a fund has to do is declare that it's going to distribute 15% annually and then pay out some combination of dividends, interest, option premiums, capital gains, or even a return of capital to make it happen.

Can it generate the option income to support the distribution? The answer also appears to be yes.

A couple of things lead me to this conclusion:

First, the fund is specifically managed toward this outcome. While OMAH creates a Buffett-like equity portfolio, the option strategy will adjust according to market conditions. If the VIX is low, it might use slightly higher beta stocks to write options on and vice versa. The dynamic nature of the strategy allows it to target a specific yield.

Second, if you look at the distribution composition of OMAH to the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD), it's nearly identical. XYLD has a years-long history of distributing steady, high monthly distributions. If the composition of those monthly payouts is substantially the same (currently about 7% dividend income, 93% option income), OMAH is very likely on the path of sustainability.

This is ideal in terms of assessing whether or not OMAH is an attractive high yield income vehicle. There's some risk in needing to rely on higher beta stocks occasionally to achieve that target yield, but the fact that the underlying portfolio consists mostly of durable, well-established, and mature names that are built to ride out varying economic cycles means the risk should be minimal.

If you're comfortable with the risk/reward profile of covered call strategies, OMAH is worth consideration.

How The OMAH ETF Fits Into Your Portfolio

Again, it all depends on your objective. Pure income seekers might feel comfortable allocating a larger portion of their portfolio to OMAH as a way to generate high income as well as capture exposure to a portfolio of high quality "Buffett-approved" companies.

For longer-term investors and/or those looking to augment a stock-heavy portfolio, OMAH probably works better in smaller quantities. If you think the current rally in U.S. stocks is about to pause or you want to hedge a bit more defensively toward income, OMAH could be a good option, especially since the underlying portfolio is full of high quality names and not just a single high volatility tech company like Palantir Technologies (PLTR) or Coinbase (COIN).

Conclusion

The idea of combining Berkshire Hathaway with a 15% yield will no doubt be appealing to a large section of income seekers. It probably won't draw nearly the attention that some of these single stock covered call ETFs with 100% yields will, but OMAH is a more sustainable, longer-term strategy that helps keep risk in check in the pursuit of high yield.

OMAH has already amassed nearly $600 million in AUM in just under 7 months since its inception, so the demand for a product like this is clearly high. If you're looking for a high income strategy for your portfolio, you could certainly do worse than investing like Warren Buffett!

data.title: Erasca: Hype Building Into The End Of The Year (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

2025 has been a cornucopia of massive updates for companies exploring new possibilities in targeting the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway for cancer. I am notably excited about the prospects of companies like Revolution Medicines, Inc. (RVMD), but I also covered Erasca, Inc. (NASDAQ:ERAS) for the first time back in April, noting a Buy sentiment on the basis of favorable capitalization relative to their assets, as well as the expected catalysts for the rest of the year. So far, this has appeared to be a prescient call, and I want to take an updated look at the company to see what we can expect next.

Pipeline Updates

Naporafenib

At the time of my last article, the lone pipeline project for ERAS was naporafenib, a small molecule inhibitor targeting different variants of the RAF kinase, with the hope of avoiding certain complications of treatment with approved agents like encorafenib and dabrafenib that have been a challenge for BRAF-mutated solid tumors. We've seen some preliminary evidence of activity for naporafenib in the setting of NRAS-mutated melanoma, as I covered previously, and ERAS was communicating further readouts in 2025.

However, back in May, the company announced that they would be pursuing development of other agents more aggressively, and they would be placing naporafenib into the category of "partnership opportunities," signaling deprioritization of this project. To date, no news has been shared on the status of these ongoing strategic discussions, and ERAS has declined to focus on sharing further clinical readouts from their studies involving naporafenib.

ERAS-4001

On the heels of this strategic reprioritization, ERAS has moved to bring the pan-KRAS inhibitor ERAS-4001 into the clinic. This agent binds to both wild-type KRAS (i.e., unmutated) and certain mutants (notably those where the G12 amino acid has been switched for something else), preventing the binding of RAF to KRAS and reducing downstream signaling in preclinical models of various RAS-associated cancers (e.g., lung cancer, pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer). It appears to bind poorly to NRAS and HRAS, which are RAS homologs that play important roles in normal cell biology.

It's worth noting that this mechanism of action is very, very different from the pan-RAS "molecular glue" approach being pursued by the likes of RVMD with daraxonrasib. An IND was cleared to begin study of ERAS-4001 in humans back in June. BOREALIS-1 is the planned phase 1 trial in KRAS-mutated solid tumors that has yet to officially begin, and ERAS intends to share preliminary data sometime in 2026.

ERAS-0015

ERAS is not stopping with a selective KRAS binder. They have also in-licensed their own "molecular glue" agent that preferentially targets the "active" forms of RAS, with broader potential inactivation of aberrant RAS genes regardless of mutational status. Early preclinical evidence suggests it may even be more persistent than daraxonrasib, with compelling antitumor activity in mouse models of colon and pancreatic cancers.

An IND to begin phase 1 studies for ERAS-0015 was cleared in May. The phase 1 AURORAS-1 study will evaluate the safety and efficacy of ERAS-0015 in RAS-mutant solid tumors, with anticipated data coming in 2026.

Financial Overview

In the latest quarterly filing, ERAS reported $66.1 million in cash and equivalents, as well as another $234.5 million in short-term marketable securities. With long-term marketable securities of $86.1 million, ERAS had total liquidity of approximately $386.7 million. Meanwhile, operating expenses for the quarter reached $38.1 million, and after other income, the net loss was $33.9 million.

Actual cash burn for the first 6 months of 2025 was $52.1 million ($20.5 million in Q2). At this cash burn rate, ERAS has a cash runway of approximately 14 quarters, consistent with management guidance of having funding "into the second half of 2028."

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Well capitalized to make the switch into a potentially red-hot space

With a cash runway spanning years (assuming that costs can be kept under control), ERAS has the space it needs to both pursue their own RAS inhibitors and drum up some excitement while riding a potential wave of enthusiasm for RAS inhibitors that we're starting to see. I wouldn't get too far ahead of being excited about their liquid holdings, since they have a lot of proving to do, but this is a decisively positive feature of ERAS right now.

Risk - The "red-hot" space comes with daunting competition far ahead of their pipeline

RVMD's molecular gluing agent, daraxonrasib, is fast on pace to complete enrollment of a phase 3 trial in pancreatic cancer. ERAS is poised to begin a first-in-human study for 2 different compounds. The fact is that ERAS is years and years behind companies it would like to be competing with, so if they end up going it alone, they may find themselves without a clear niche.

Risk - No guarantees for naporafenib partnership of the success of their RAS inhibitors

Moreover, the strategic discussions they're having related to naporafenib have yet to materialize into a partnership, and we have not seen a strong indication that anyone is willing to bite. Beyond that, we still do not know whether these pan-RAS inhibitors will ultimately be feasible and effective, ERAS's entries included.

And they boast "best-in-class" potential for ERA-0015 (the RAS molecular glue), but higher potency does not necessarily translate into a better drug, especially when your target is so widely expressed and crucial throughout different tissues in the body.

Bottom Line Summary

At a market cap of over $650 million at the time of writing, it's safe to say that ERAS is not being ignored by the market. I would attribute this to the general excitement we're seeing regarding RAS-targeted agents. And that could spell major upside for the company, since success from companies like RVMD could trigger a buying spree for smaller developers of these agents.

However, since the company has doubled in value in just the past few months, supported by no clear catalysts, I am inclined to temper my expectations for the near-term prospects of ERAS. I am revising my sentiment at this time to a Hold, given the risks involved with bringing any drug through early-stage clinical trials. At the rate they're going, ERAS will likely not report interim data until well into 2026, and I am not willing to count on a buyout for any company as part of the investment thesis.

One who has a high risk tolerance could be justified in rolling the dice with this one, but for now, I would put ERAS on a high-interest watchlist rather than jumping in with both feet. I expect there will be a better entry point for the stock.

data.title: Rolls-Royce: Market Valuations Don't Reflect The Nuclear Energy Potential
data.description:
For Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) (OTCPK:RYCEF), nuclear energy might yet be a minuscule part of its business, but make no mistake, it has massive potential. What with all the policy push towards accelerating its development as a clean and reliable source of energy?

No less than seven pure-play nuclear energy stocks have more than doubled YTD. The best performing, the fast reactor developer Oklo (OKLO), is up by over 1,000%. Rolls-Royce stock, too, has done well, to be sure, more than doubling so far in 2025. But here I discuss how the full potential of its nuclear energy business isn't captured in the valuations.

Growing focus on nuclear energy

Rolls-Royce's nuclear energy business needs to be looked at more carefully because of the growing focus on the sector. And indeed, the company's potential role in it, not just in the UK but also across the borders. Specifically from the company's perspective, two key developments have ensued recently that highlight the rising importance of nuclear energy.

#1. U.S.-UK partnership on nuclear energy

Earlier this year, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright called for a "nuclear renaissance." The statement is now almost mirrored by his UK counterpart, Ed Miliband, who has said, "We are kickstarting the golden age of nuclear in this country..." This statement came as the two countries' recently entered into a partnership on nuclear energy. The key goals of the partnership are as follows:

  • Fast-tracking of reactor design checks is envisaged. This means, one country's process can get supported by the other's, if it has approved of them.
  • Workload sharing on-site licensing is another goal of the partnership.
  • Collaborating on nuclear fusion's development is also seen, even though it's well understood that it will take a few decades for the process to commercialize.

While nuclear energy's contribution to Rolls-Royce's business is so small that it now gets covered in the "all other businesses" division, recently, the company has made a significant dent in the industry. It won the UK government's Great British Nuclear (GBN) competition, which means it will build three small modular reactors (SMRs) in the UK (see subhead #3 under the heading '3 catalysts for Rolls-Royce' of the link for details).

SMRs might have found few takers in the past, but they are now being seen as potentially the future of nuclear energy, as they can enable its rapid development as energy security is underlined along with the target of net-zero emissions by 2050.

Before this can happen, though, as a United States International Trade Commission paper notes, "This will involve continued research, collaborative initiatives, regulatory reforms, and public engagement." To this extent, the discussed U.S.-UK partnership is a step in the right direction.

#2. Speculation over nuclear unit IPO as business ramps up

With all the buzz around nuclear energy, the Financial Times reported that Rolls-Royce could publicly list its nuclear energy business in August. The company has since denied it, but is said to be exploring funding options. At any rate, an IPO down the line could well still be possible.

This is hardly surprising considering that, besides the GBN win, the company has made progress on two more fronts:

  • It has entered into a collaboration with the Czech energy utility, ČEZ Group, to supply six SMRs to them. Incidentally, the ČEZ Group also has a 20% stake in Rolls-Royce SMR, the reactor design arm and subsidiary of the company.
  • Further, the company could also become Sweden's partner in meeting its nuclear energy expansion objectives. At present it's in competition with GE Vernova (GEV), with the final outcome yet to be decided.

Estimating nuclear business's potential contribution

In a BBC article, the company's CEO, Tufan Erginbilgic, estimates that each SMR will cost up to $3 billion. With the three SMRs to be supplied after winning the GBN and another six as part of its deal with the ČEZ Group, it suggests that the segment can earn up to $27 billion for Rolls-Royce in the next years.

Of course it's likely that the company can garner more deals, but for the sake of this analysis, let's assume that this is the extent of its SMR business for now. The on-site construction time for an SMR is four years, as per the company.

Suppose the entire process from start to end takes five years, and the revenues are split equally across the years, with the process starting from 2026, the company can earn $5.4 billion or £4.15 billion per year from the nuclear energy business from 2026-2030.

Let me contextualize this. Analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha put the company's revenues at $28.9 billion (£22.25 billion) in 2026. With this as the case, the SMR business will bring in 18.7% of the business, though it dwindles to 13.8% by 2030.

But that's only assuming that the segment doesn't grow. In actual fact, the company expects that some 400 SMRs will be required by 2050, which means the SMR market size will be around $1.2 trillion. Rolls-Royce wants to dominate this business. Even if Rolls-Royce were to get 15% of it, there's $120 billion in revenues to be made, which means $7.2 billion per year up to 2050 and likely more considering its credentials.

Even sticking to the contracted business, though, for now, the annual revenue can put the segment close to the company's second and third biggest revenue-generating segments, Defence and Power Systems, which each brought in between £4-4.5 billion last year. The mainstay, however, is expected to continue being the Civil Aerospace segment, which brought in over half the revenues in 2024.

Re-valuing Rolls Royce in the context of SMRs

Based on my earlier estimates for the underlying net profit, which remain unchanged, the forward non-GAAP P/E for RYCEY stands at 36.19x, which is lower than the stock's five-year average of 42.23x. This implies over 15% upside even right now.

This isn't even taking into account the skyrocketing valuations for pure-play reactor developers, driven by big tech taking a shine to nuclear energy to meet massive projected demand for electricity driven by the AI boom. A key example of this is the majority nuclear energy utility Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG) entering into a power supply deal with Microsoft (MSFT) last year, which will result in the re-opening of its Three Mile Island nuclear plant in Pennsylvania.

With this as the background, the SMR developer NuScale Power (SMR) is right now trading at a forward P/S of 112.3x (it's not profit-making yet, so the P/E doesn't come into the equation). Oklo, which becomes revenue-making only in 2029, is trading at a forward P/S of 250.9x for the year. Similarly, micro-reactor developer NANO Nuclear Energy (NNE), which expected to become revenue-generating in earnest by 2027, is trading at 253.9x for the year.

For the purpose of re-estimating Rolls-Royce's fair valuations, here it's assumed that the earlier estimated 18.7% SMR revenues will show up from 2026. Next, the fair forward P/S-based valuation based on the multiples for reactor developers is assumed to be 100x, just to keep it slightly conservative. The fair valuation for the rest of the business is assumed to be the stock's five-year average. This results in an upgraded fair valuation of 54.78x, implying over 50% upside to the stock.

What next?

Recent developments point to the growing focus on nuclear energy, which Rolls-Royce is poised to benefit from. It has proven its merit by winning the GBN and even contracts for SMRs. But the stock's valuations don't reflect the substantial market multiples seen for pure-play reactor developers.

Baking in the potential for the company's SMR business into these valuations reflects significant upside to RYCEY even now. But here's the rub. Even without it, my earnings estimates still indicate a healthy upside. I'm retaining a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hallador Energy Strengthens Its Position In The Face Of Demand For Power-Hungry Data Centers (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A Buy for Hallador Energy Company

This analysis contains a Buy rating for NASDAQ-listed shares of Hallador Energy Company (NASDAQ:HNRG), reflecting an improvement from our previous article dated April 23, 2023.

About Hallador Energy Company

In its results for the second quarter of 2025, Hallador Energy Company describes itself under a specific section as follows:

Hallador Energy Company is a vertically-integrated Independent Power Producer (IPP) based in Terre Haute, Indiana. The Company has two core businesses:

Hallador Power Company, LLC, which produces electricity and capacity at its one Gigawatt (GW) Merom Generating Station, and Sunrise Coal, LLC, which produces and supplies fuel to the Merom Generating Station and other companies.

Source: “Hallador Energy Company Reports Second Quarter 2025 Financial and Operating Results”, August 11, 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha, originally released by Hallador Energy Company in TERRE HAUTE, via the GLOBE NEWSWIRE

Hereinafter, “Hallador Energy Company” is referred to as “HNRG”.

HNRG Stock Performance Since Our Last Article

Since our last article on April 23, 2023, HNRG's NASDAQ-listed shares have seen a positive change of 120.64% and outperformed the S&P 500 by 62.51% at the time of writing. The positive catalysts we anticipated in our previous analysis have all played out as expected, leading to a higher market value for the stock on the NASDAQ. The commissioning of the Indiana power plant plan through a cost-efficient and dynamic structure enabled HNRG to gain a foothold in the energy market. The company expands its commercial presence beyond coal, creating an opportunity to capitalize on a new era of high demand for energy.

The Outlook Is Promising For HNRG

The economy will require more energy to make its AI-powered data centers and power-driven means of transport operational, implement electrification, and develop other strategies for greater sustainability and security for its own population, such as the transition to a green economy and strengthening self-defense capabilities against potential external threats or attacks. The market trend seems to be for natural gas to cover a large portion of energy needs, which are expected to double for data centers alone by 2028, according to the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, as reported in this Yahoo Finance article. Analysts, banks, and corporate executives agree that natural gas is cheap, readily available, and relatively clean. Natural gas is essentially the best resource to meet the growing need for reliable base-load power. The emerging trend in the energy market is presenting more opportunities for adding dispatchable generation through the installation of natural gas capacity at Merom, which means that HNRG has to co-fire the energy plant in Indiana. HNRG could seize this opportunity if, in its current commercial strategy of entering into long-term power purchase agreements (“PPAs”), it finds valuable customers among utilities and data center developers. Matching the identikit will be those customers, some of whom have a vested interest in ensuring their energy use has no or minimal environmental impact, who could create value for HNRG shareholders as the company has, of course, to weigh the impact on its balance sheet of the costs of transitioning to co-firing and the necessary financing.

According to Earnings Call Insights: Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) Q2 2025, Brent K. Bilsland, President, CEO & Chairman of HNRG, stated (In “Management View”):

The ultimate decision of whether to co-fire, when to implement the change and the associated cost and funding in connection with such a change is inherently dependent on the type of long-term PPA transaction, if any, that we ultimately reach.

We are in the process of gathering and evaluating multiple offers from a variety of sources, including utilities and data center developers.

Source: “Hallador Energy outlines long-term PPA momentum and evaluates dual-fuel strategy amid rising inventory and strong coal shipments”, August 11, 2025, Earnings Call Insights: Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) Q2 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha

Regarding long-term PPA momentum, Brent K. Bilsland added (In “Outlook”), Brent K. Bilsland stated:

We remain optimistic that these conversations will culminate in a long-term agreement or agreements that enhance shareholder value.

Source: “Hallador Energy outlines long-term PPA momentum and evaluates dual-fuel strategy amid rising inventory and strong coal shipments”, August 11, 2025, Earnings Call Insights: Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) Q2 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha

On the long-term momentum of PPAs (in “Q&A”): When asked by Nicholas Giles of B. Riley Securities about his openness to multiple arrangements instead of client concentration and the end markets served by counterparties, Bilsland stated:

These are all investment-grade counterparties ultimately that I think would be our customer.

Source: “Hallador Energy outlines long-term PPA momentum and evaluates dual-fuel strategy amid rising inventory and strong coal shipments”, August 11, 2025, Earnings Call Insights: Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) Q2 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha

Hallador Energy's Operational and Financial Performance: A Look at Second Quarter 2025 Results and Trends

The energy market is naturally subject to fluctuations, influenced by macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, among others, but especially by the weather, which in turn affects household energy consumption. HNRG, like any other energy company, has no control over these factors and can do nothing other than accept the conditions imposed by the market. This includes the crucial condition of the price at which HNRG can sell its energy to customers and its coal to third parties. However, HNRG is a vertically integrated company. If the coal business is managed according to the best possible economic criteria, allowing for a certain degree of volatility, this resilience should also extend to the energy trading business. This provides immunization to the company's earnings against economic headwinds that could temporarily coincide with lower-than-expected energy prices, such as in the second quarter of 2025. Even the outage at one of HNRG's Merom generating units, albeit planned by the company, coinciding with the “seasonal spring softness in the energy market,” as Brent Bilsland calls it in his comments on the Q2 2025 results, does not appear to be a headwind that could jeopardize the operational robustness of an entire structure. By drawing vitality from a strong coal mining operation capable of becoming even stronger through improved cost efficiency and higher recovery rates (qualities mentioned in our previous article as potential upside catalysts), this structure thus achieves significant profitability across the board, as illustrated in this company's screenshot of Q2 2025 results.

Thus,

  • Total Sales and Operating Revenue increased 9.7% year-over-year to $102.9 million in the second quarter of 2025. This was driven by third-party coal sales of $38.1 million, a significant increase of 16.2% year-over-year, while electricity sales of $60 million remained stable year-over-year, despite headwinds from the planned shutdown of HNRG's Merom power plant and seasonal weakness in the spring energy market.
  • Net income for the second quarter of 2025 was $8.2 million, compared to a loss of $10.2 million in the second quarter of 2024.
  • The company generated operating cash flow of $11.4 million in the second quarter of 2025, which partially covered capital expenditures of $13.1 million (compared to $13.2 million in the same period last year). The operating cash flow represents a decrease of 51.5% compared to the prior year. However, this negative development was temporary and is attributable to the hike in the inventory, which reached $43.57 million in the second quarter of 2025 (compared to $32.595 million in the same quarter last year). The higher inventory reflects cash outflows associated with the purchase of production inputs and the additional costs of manufacturing the commodities now held in inventory, which will only be recovered through cash inflows from the sale of these commodities. But there is also a positive aspect to the whole matter, and that is that the stronger-than-expected jump in inventories promises positive effects in the coming quarters.

Brent Bilsland, CEO of HNRG, stated:

“[…] As a result of these operational enhancements and our planned outage at Merom, inventory levels rose in the quarter, positioning us for an active second half as both units return to full dispatch and coal customer shipments accelerate.”

Source: “Hallador Energy Company Reports Second Quarter 2025 Financial and Operating Results”, August 11, 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha, originally released by Hallador Energy Company in TERRE HAUTE, Indiana, via GLOBE NEWSWIRE.

  • Lastly, adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter of 2025 was $3.5 million, compared to a loss of $5.8 million in the second quarter of 2024.

The Financial Condition Is Solid To Support Current Operations and Growth Initiatives

The financial situation is strong enough to support HNRG's current operational status and the development of its power purchase agreements (“PPAs”), and what these imply in relation to potentially co-firing with natural gas, for example, at its Merom power plant in Indiana.

Total liquidity was $42 million as of June 30, 2025, compared to $69 million as of March 31, 2025, primarily due to the contribution from operating cash flow, which this time was lower, reflected in an increase in inventory, but as seen, paves the way for higher liquidity in the coming quarters. Total liquidity was $42 million as of June 30, 2025, an improvement from the $37.8 million that HNRG ended 2024 with.

Total bank debt was $45 million as of June 30, 2025, compared to $23 million as of March 31, 2025, but we expect a downward trend and a convergence with the previous quarter, as the planned maintenance outage resulted in higher revolving debt in the second quarter of 2025. Nevertheless, the company's debt remained within budget in the second quarter of 2025, compared to the debt level of $44 million that HNRG ended 2024 with.

Let's now examine some of the key metrics that underpin this solid balance sheet. Their drivers are:

Profitability drivers: Robust contracted electricity and coal revenues for at least the next two years (see screenshot of the second quarter 2025 results below).

Furthermore, as indicated by the CEO, a return to full utilization of the improved power generation assets and an acceleration of coal deliveries to third parties are expected.

Also, most recently, on September 9, 2025, the U.S. Energy Information Administration (“EIA”) painted a more optimistic outlook for electricity prices in the coming years in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (“STEO”) on electricity prices, their trends, and related forecasts.

The EIA stated:

Electricity generation. Electricity generation has been growing rapidly this year as a result of growing demand for power from data centers and industrial customers. We expect that total U.S. generation by the electric power sector will grow by 2.3% in 2025 and a further 3.0% next year. […]

Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) website, accessed October 2, 2025, EIA publication "Short-Term Energy Outlook," published September 9, 2025.

As we understand it, there is a tsunami of energy demand on the horizon (we previously mentioned Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory’s estimate in a Yahoo Finance article) from data center developers and various electrification initiatives, and that means electricity prices will be on the rise. Significant upgrades to the grid infrastructure are needed to support data center developers and other electrification initiatives, as well as, for example, charging infrastructure for electric vehicles, and the implication of this is that the future price of electricity will also have to cover the cost of upgrading the infrastructure. Natural gas will be in high demand because this energy source, due to its previously mentioned properties such as large availability, lower development costs, and environmental sustainability, will provide reliable base-load power to support a massive increase in energy demand in the coming years.

The only flaw in the EIA's forecasts lies in its short-term coal price projections. These predict a decline in coal exports in 2025 and 2026 as a persistent global coal glut pushes down coal prices. This temporarily deviates from the long-term projections by EIA, which in 2023 showed that the price of this commodity would generally rise over the next few decades.

The EIA here says:

The outlook for coal prices in the United States

In the Annual Energy Outlook 2023 Reference case, the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects that U.S. coal prices will generally increase each year through 2050, based largely on certain assumptions about the coal and electricity markets and industrial sector demand.

Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) website, accessed October 2, 2025, EIA: “Coal Explained - Coal Prices and Outlook,” last updated April 17, 2024.

In our view, the EIA's long-term projections are highly reliable at this point in time – that is, after an analysis of all available information. Because: it is true that renewable energy sources such as solar and wind power, whose “increasing capacity of power generation,” according to Trading Economics (at the time of this writing), “more than met the demand growth for electricity in the world's largest manufacturing sector,” are ultimately putting pressure that explains the lower price of coal. But solar and wind power lack the same constancy of energy supply that ensures system stability as coal does. It is no coincidence that coal, along with nuclear power and natural gas, generates the majority of America's electricity.

Given the enormous electricity demand of data centers, as well as electrification and the use of electric vehicles, we are in line with those who expect a positive future for thermal coal.

HNRG's outlook fits well with these outlooks for electricity and coal, as evidenced by how it is described by the CEO:

The CEO stated, "Beginning in 2026, our average contracted sales prices across both Hallador and Sunrise Coal step up meaningfully compared to current levels. With respect to energy sales, our largest PPA contract will see an increase of more than $20 per megawatt hour in 2026 as compared to 2025 on expected volumes of approximately 1.6 million megawatt hours."

He also noted that "our average contracted sales price across all contracts in 2026 is approximately $4 per ton higher than the average contracted sales price in 2025."

Source: “Hallador Energy outlines long-term PPA momentum and evaluates dual-fuel strategy amid rising inventory and strong coal shipments”, August 11, 2025, Earnings Call Insights: Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) Q2 2025, reported by Seeking Alpha

Drivers for Improved Leverage Conditions: Rising profitability generates more liquidity and thus strengthens the balance sheet. We also discuss the development of the debt structure and its impact on credit conditions, as these things have a positive impact on key financial leverage metrics such as the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio and interest coverage.

In June 2025, Hallador amended its credit agreement to increase operating flexibility for the remainder of the year. By depositing $19 million into a money market account from a $35 million prepaid power purchase agreement, the scheduled debt repayment from October 2025 was postponed to January 2026. The company may have identified an alternative refinancing solution that can be implemented, starting with the fixed repayment schedule expiring throughout 2026. In our view, the interest burden should now be on track to gradually decrease as the Federal Reserve is expected to further reduce borrowing costs following the 25 basis point rate cut on September 17, 2025, due to the weakening labor market.

The following financial leverage ratios may get better and better, which bodes well for the sentiment around this stock:

  • Net Debt to Total 12-month adjusted EBITDA Ratio = $46.22 million to $38.5 million = 1.20x, which is a good value. A lower ratio, perhaps below 1.0x, is preferable as it indicates a healthier financial position and lower financial risk. However, given that the Independent Power Producers and Energy Traders sector is a capital-intensive sector that requires massive investment in infrastructure and assets, 1.2x is acceptable and can be improved, as we have given various hints.

Net debt is calculated as total debt of $55.42 million as of Q2 2025 minus cash and cash equivalents of $9.2 million as of Q2 2025, equaling $46.22 million as of Q2 2025.

Total 12-month adjusted EBITDA = Q2 2025 + Q1 2025 + Q4 2024 + Q3 2024 = $3.4 Million + $19.3 Million + $6.2 Million + $9.6 Million = $38.5 Million.

  • The 12-month June 2025 Operating Income is divided by the 12-month June 2025 Interest Expense, total, and the result is = $30.2 million to $13.7 million = 2.20x, which is consistent with an Interest Coverage Ratio of at least 1.5 to 2.0x, which is generally considered acceptable. Higher than 2.2x is always better, but for the Independent Power Producers and Energy Traders sector, which typically involves substantial long-term debt, 2.2x may be seen as reasonable.

HNRG Hallador Energy Company on NASDAQ: Valuation

At the time of this article, Hallador Energy Company's stock was trading on the NASDAQ under the ticker symbol “HNRG” at $18.71 per share and had a market capitalization of $858.05 million.

The shares are trading relatively high compared to the 52-week range of $8.13 to $22.01 per share and are also above the MA Ribbon. The RSI indicates that the shares are currently neither overbought nor oversold.

But it seems that momentum is building, temporarily pushing the stock into a bearish phase to take advantage of a lower market price. There are potential upsides to this stock, which we have previously explored in this analysis, but we also have the following valuation of HNRG relative to its potential competitors, which may suggest fair valuations even at these levels:

HNRG has an EV/EBITDA (“TTM”) of 10.16x versus a sector median of 12.43x, according to Seeking Alpha, which rather suggests that this stock may not currently be worse valued than many other competitors in the “Independent Power Producers and Energy Traders” space, although the trends observed earlier in the chart may make the situation appear somewhat different.

Conclusion

HNRG's promising prospects, with upside potential from an improved platform as electricity sales and prices increase, could encourage investors to add more shares to their long-term investment horizons. Longer-term, this will also benefit from rising coal prices and a potential transition to natural gas co-firing at the Merom, Indiana, power plant.

data.title: Gold: Get Out Now, But Buy The Dip
data.description:
Gold has been in a sweet spot for a long time now. In fact, I even called it a "No-Brainer Investment," right before it bottomed around $1,600 in 2022. I invested heavily in the segment then, and it remained one of my most significant portfolio segments in recent years. More recently, I discussed why I bought the dip in gold in May, as the yellow metal cooled off to my buy-in range, around the $3,200-3,000 support level.

Gold 1-Year Chart

Gold has surged by approximately 140% since its 2022 bottom, and is up by roughly 25% since my most recent update in May. In general, gold is having a spectacular year, up by roughly 50% from its 52-week low, which is better than the S&P 500's 40% meteoric rise from the April bottom.

Also, it's not just gold. I noted several stocks in my "Gold: The Buying Opportunity Of The Year Has Arrived" article. I published this article right around the lows last November as the strong dollar was temporarily weighing gold down. I highlighted my top three gold mining stocks in the article: Newmont Corp. (NEM), Barrick Mining (B), and Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM). Newmont was around $41 then, Barrick was at approximately $17, and AEM was at about $77. I provided relatively conservative 12-month price target ranges then, which was just fewer than 11 months ago now.

Given that these were issued approximately 11 months ago, gold and my top gold mining holdings have done even better than I expected. Gold futures hit a record high above $3,900 in recent sessions, roughly a 50% increase. NEM surged from about $40 to around $88, a 120% increase. Barrick Mining went from $17 to approximately $36, roughly a 110% increase. And AEM also surged by a very impressive 120%, eclipsing my estimates.

Now, Gold Could Use A Pullback

Gold's and the gold miners' performance have been outstanding. However, trees don't grow to the sky, and stocks shouldn't move up in a straight line without corrections.

While I remain bullish on gold and gold mining stocks in the intermediate and longer term, in the near term we may see a notable pullback. Due to this dynamic, I've decreased some of my gold- and silver-related holdings in recent weeks, as we may witness more volatility and profit-taking in the near term here.

Newmont Corp. 1-Year Chart

Newmont is the most significant gold miner globally, and is one of my favorite gold mining stocks. It's also been and remains a core position in my all-weather portfolio.

However, the stock has become extremely overbought recently. The RSI has been above 70 for about six weeks now. The stock is about 56% above its 20-day MA, and the full stochastic dipped below 80 for the first time in about two months.

YTD, Newmont's stock has appreciated by nearly 140%, which is spectacular. However, Newmont is a gold mining company, and not a rapidly growing AI stock. The technical image implies that Newmont may be around a near-term high point, momentum is decelerating, and the probability for a correction is relatively high.

While it's still not expensive given its 15 forward P/E ratio, it can certainly use a technical pullback, in my view, and I would look to increase my Newmont position around the $75-70 range.

Barrick Mining 1-Year Chart

Barrick is another gold miner that I have been very keen on, and I had a very significant position in this stock before trimming it recently. The RSI here actually hit about 90, which is extremely high, and we've seen the stock making lower highs on extremely high volume recently. The full stochastic also dipped below 80 recently.

This dynamic implies that momentum is worsening, the stock may be running out of potential buyers at these levels, and we could see the market flip in the near term. By flip, I mean that long investors may stop buying and take more profits, and the smart money as well as the algos may be shorting this stock already, and could increase short positions in the near term.

Barrick surged by about 140% from its 52-week low, and the price action is about 70% above its 200-day MA, which is extremely high and may not be sustainable. Barrick is also not expensive, with a 2026 forward P/E of about 13, but it is very overbought technically. Therefore, I will look for buying opportunities in the $30-26 zone.

Kinross Gold Corp. 1-Year Chart

Kinross is another solid gold stock I've been very bullish on over the past year or so. This stock has climbed by about 180% from its low point around late last year.

Here, we can also see obvious overbought indicators flashing warning signals. The RSI has been making lower highs despite the price action making new highs lately, which is a negative technical divergence.

The stock is about 70% above its 200-day MA, and we see a possible blowoff top occurring as we speak. Kinross is also relatively cheap from a fundamental perspective, as it trades at about a 14 forward P/E ratio. Nonetheless, the stock can use a technical pullback, and I would be willing to increase my position around the $22-20 point.

The Bottom Line: Get Ready To Buy The Dip

While I expect a near-term pullback/correction and some turbulence in the near term in the gold market, I remain bullish on gold and high quality gold miners in the intermediate and longer term.

After all, as we advance into 2026, the Fed's easing cycle could accelerate, providing additional tailwinds for gold and high-quality gold mining stocks. Therefore, gold could go considerably higher in 2026 and beyond.

While I am short-term negative on gold miners due to overextended technical conditions, valuations appear reasonable and even cheap in some regard. So, I have decreased some of my gold-related holdings, but I am preparing to buy the dip in future weeks.

data.title: Bitcoin: The Final Rally Before The Crash (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has once again reclaimed the $120,000 area as we enter what could be the most bullish month of the year.

However, the Bitcoin 4-year cycle is nearing its end, suggesting Bitcoin could be topping in the next run-up.

Ultimately, though, I believe global liquidity will determine if we can head even higher for longer.

Nonetheless, as we reach my long-term Bitcoin target, I do think this could be a good time to de-risk from Bitcoin.

While I remain bullish long-term, I am downgrading Bitcoin to a Hold due to these reasons.

Uptober And The 4-Year Cycle

Historically speaking, October has been the best month for Bitcoin in terms of price appreciation, hence the coining of the term Uptober.

With not that much to go on in terms of traditional fundamentals, crypto investors often look at things like seasonality.

This brings me to my next point, which is the fact that we are approaching the end of the Bitcoin 4-year cycle.

This 4-year cycle also coincides with the dates of the Bitcoin halving, which usually precedes a big price rally, followed by a top, bear market, consolidation, and then the next halving, which restarts the cycle.

Bitcoin’s last halving was on April 19th, 2024. If history is about to repeat itself, then we should see Bitcoin top anywhere from 12-18 months following this halving. Ultimately, this means Bitcoin’s four-year cycle could complete in Q4 of 2025.

But although the halving cycle has served us well in the past, I think the real mover of Bitcoin is the liquidity cycle, which has just happened to coincide with the 4-year halving cycle.

What’s Next For Liquidity?

As we can see, Bitcoin has moved in line with Global M2 for the last decade.

The tops in 2018 and 2022 also coincided with liquidity and market tops.

So the real question here is: what’s next for liquidity?

Over the past few months, we have benefited from some favourable liquidity dynamics.

  • PBoC liquidity injections

  • Weaker USD

  • Lower bond volatility

  • Fiscal stimulus

As we approach 2026, this is something that could change.

Japan and the US continue to taper, though the Fed has now begun cutting rates. China’s PBoC should continue to support markets as it expands liquidity. And the dollar looks set to keep sliding.

Overall, it looks like countries around the world are intent on keeping stimulus in line with the dynamics I’ve mentioned before of monetary debasement necessary to deliver global debt.

The Two Big Risk Factors

As we approach 2026, though, there are two big risk factors that could derail markets.

  • AI Recession

  • Inflation

The outlook for liquidity depends on what Central Banks and markets do. My biggest worry, right now, is that as Central Banks like the Fed cut into an already hot economy, inflation could spark another 2022-style selloff in equities and crypto.

On the other hand, an AI recession could take the market down in the other direction, through deflation. Expectations are sky high right now, perhaps with good reason, but this could begin to unwind in the other direction as growth and valuations peak.

In my opinion, the biggest bottleneck right now is power, with today’s power grids potentially not being ready to accommodate the sudden increase in AI demand.

This means even if AI efficiencies are real, it’s possible that data centers are going to be overbuilt, to the extent that not all of them might be able to operate given energy limitations.

Bitcoin Technical Analysis

All in all, there are various risk factors lining up, which, paired with the dynamics of the 4-year cycle and the fact that we are reaching important technical levels, make me wary about Bitcoin over the next six months.

This Bitcoin rally is running on fumes.

Note that we are having trouble breaking through the resistance from the green trendline drawn from the 2021 top, which also lines up quite well with the 2 ext of the bear market.

On the other hand, we are forming a huge RSI divergence on the weekly chart.

In my view, if we managed to reach the 2.618 ext at $150,000, paired with the RSI entering overbought and touching that trendline we have drawn on the indicator, this would be enough for me to reduce exposure significantly to Bitcoin.

On the other hand, a break below the 50-week EMA now, which sits at $100,000, would be an initial indication that momentum has turned decidedly bearish

Final Thoughts

Bitcoin has increased more than 8x since its bear market lows, and it could 10x if we reach the $150,000 level outlined above.

With mounting risks in terms of liquidity, AI hype reaching a fever pitch, and technicals looking shaky, I think we could be near the end of this Bitcoin bull run.

What happens next will depend on the actions of Central banks, but either way, I think it’s time to take some chips off the table.

data.title: 3M's Restructuring Delivers Richer Margins And Renewed Growth Opportunities
data.description:
MMM's Ongoing Renewal Of Its Aging Portfolio Delivers Robust Growth Prospects

I previously covered 3M (NYSE:MMM) in July 2025, discussing how the stock's correction after the double-beat FQ2'25 performance and the raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance implied a notable profit taking at prior heights, worsened by the uncertainties arising from the mixed macroeconomic environment, the ongoing tariff war, and the softer auto/consumer electronics end market.

Despite so, I had believed that the stock remained highly compelling then, attributed to the renewed growth opportunities through strategic product launches in the semiconductor/ aerospace end market, the ongoing productivity efforts, and the raised consensus forward estimates through FY2027.

MMM 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating nearer to the intermediate 2025 support levels of $144s, my Buy Zone has materialized by early August 2025, as MMM also rallies by +8% since then while outperforming its industrial peers by large degrees.

The ongoing bullish support is unsurprising indeed, attributed to the robust recovery prospects of its three business groups, most notably in the Safety & Industrial segment at an organic growth profile of +2.6% YoY in FQ2'25, with it building upon the tougher YoY comparison at +1.1% YoY in FQ2'24.

These reasons may also why MMM has felt confident to offer a robust FY2025 organic growth guidance of +2% YoY, adj operating margin of 23.15% at the midpoint (+1.75 points YoY), and adj EPS of $7.875 (+7.8% YoY).

These numbers are well supported by the excellent H1'25 performance at +1.5% YoY, 24% (+2.5 points YoY), and $4.04 (+10.9% YoY) as well, respectively, with a further beat and raise performance highly possible in the upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call sometime in October 2025.

MMM's outperformance is not accidental as well, with it arising from the excellent new product launches of 126 units in H1'25 (+65.7% YoY), in an attempt to drive renewed growth opportunities through new innovations against the previously aging portfolio.

Given the increased focus on higher growth markets like auto electrification, industrial automation, data centers/ semis, and climate tech, it is unsurprising that the company has been able to report rich new product sales growth at +9% YoY in H1'25 as the management also hints at +15% YoY growth in FY2025, with it marking an end to its bottoming 5Y new product sales growth in FY2024.

Readers must note that MMM's higher sales volume from the new product launches has already been accretive to its FY2025 adj EPS at "approximately $0.50 to $0.55," partly aided by the improved productivity efforts albeit negated by the "$0.30 to $0.35 of tariff impact and higher interest expense."

This is despite the higher H1'25 R&D expenses by +7.3% YoY, with it apparent that the management has been hyper focused on delivering renewed top/ bottom-line growth while streamlining its operations, expanding its machine utilization, increasing its sales effectiveness, and improving its pricing power, all of which has been accretive to its richer bottom-lines.

The higher demand for its offerings has been observed in MMM's growing backlog at "20% to 25% coverage of third quarter sales" as well, with it lending credence to the management's promising FY2025 guidance and the higher new launch pipelines in H2'25.

Given how the ongoing restructuring efforts have driven the robust H1'25 performance while triggering the stock's recent outperformance, I urge readers to pay attention to these performance metrics in the upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call, since these are highly reflective of the company's long-term prospects.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Thanks to the excellent organic growth performance in H1'25 and the promising FY2025 guidance, I can understand why the consensus have already raised their forward estimates, with MMM expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +2.8%/ +8.1% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +2.6%/ +7.5% in April 2025, while running away from the 5Y historical growth profile at -6%/ -3.9% and the 10Y growth at -2.9%/ -0.3%, respectively, with it apparent that the Solventum (SOLV) divestiture and the intensified innovation strategies have worked as intended to drive renewed growth.

This is especially since MMM has highlighted their medium term target with FY2027 operating margins of 25% and adj EPS growth of high single digits annually in the recent 2025 Investor Day.

Most importantly, the management has also raised their FY2025 adj Free Cash Flow guidance of $5.3B at the midpoint (+8.1% YoY), with it lending strength to its secure dividend investment thesis based on the annualized dividend payouts of $1.56B while allowing them to retire -2.5% of its float over the LTM and -7.2% since FY2019.

MMM Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has already upgraded MMM's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.51x, compared to the 1Y mean of 18.46x, the 5Y mean of 15.67x, and the 10Y mean of 17.65x, thanks to the brighter long-term prospects.

The same has been observed in its more compelling 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.40x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.51x and the consensus 3Y adj EPS growth estimates of +8.1%.

When compared to the sector FWD P/E non-GAAP median valuations of 20.69x and the sector FWD PEG non-GAAP median ratio of 1.81x along with its peers, including:

  • DuPont de Nemours (DD) at 18.02x/ 2.26x and
  • Honeywell International (HON) at 19.91x/ 2.34x, respectively,

it is apparent that MMM remains rather compelling here, despite the 1Y stock price gain by +15.3% and the rally by +82.2% since the October 2023 bottom.

So, Is MMM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

MMM 10Y Stock Price

For now, MMM reversal from the Q4'23 and Q1'24 bottom has been extremely impressive indeed, with it signaling the growing bullish support surrounding its future recovery prospects after the much needed restructuring efforts.

MMM's Technical Indicators

Based on the technical indicators, MMM appears to be consolidating at current levels as well, with the moderating trading volumes and the reasonable flow index implying its potential break out after the sideways trading cadence since early 2025, assuming that historical trends persist.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.51x (near to the peer group mean of ~19x) and the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $7.875 (+7.8% YoY), it is apparent that the stock continues to trade near to my fair value estimates of $153.60.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $9.22, there remains a decent upside potential of +15.2% to my long-term price target of $179.80 as well.

MMM is likely to offer an excellent dividend raise in the near-term, with the last payout raise by +4.2% YoY in early 2025 implying a higher annualized dividend per share at $3.04 and an expanded forward dividend yield of 1.9%, compared to the sector median of 1.49%.

As a result of the reduced tariff risks arising from the raised profit guidance, the growing bullish support at current levels, the reasonable valuations, and the potential double and raise FQ3'25 performance, I am reiterating my Buy rating here.

data.title: lululemon athletica: A Contrarian Hunt In Tariff Territory
data.description:
lululemon athletica's (NASDAQ:LULU) stock drawdown is rational, as U.S. comps have turned negative, inventories are up, and management cut FY2025 revenue/EPS explicitly because of higher tariffs and the removal of the de minimis exemption. The impact of these together is likely to cut $240M from gross profit even after mitigations. That said, the company has a debt-free balance sheet with elite margins. For reference, in FY2024, Lululemon's GAAP gross margin was 59.2%, and its operating margin was 23.7%. Perhaps one of the largest drags on investor sentiment is that the company's greatest growth region is China, which was up 41% in FY2024 and 25% in Q2 FY2025; it's the weak U.S. and tariff shock that are compressing profitability.

LULU stock is undeniably cheap on the surface, but as you will know if you are an experienced value investor, a good price is not enough; we also need sentiment catalysts. The 14-week RSI is 35, which looks cheap immediately, but it has to be reinforced by the monthly RSI, as we are in a downtrend here, which is proving quite brutal. Thankfully, the 14-month RSI is 36, which shows we may be near a bottom. Typically, when fundamentals remain relatively supported (even by geopolitically sensitive international growth), a 14-month RSI of near or especially under 30 establishes "value too good to ignore," irrespective of operational imperfections (not calamities).

Fundamentals

In Q1 2025, lululemon's total net revenue increased by 7% year-over-year, but Americas comps (not new stores) revenue was down by -2%. Inventories were also up by 23% year-over-year to $1.7B, indicating a lack of sales momentum compared to management's prior expectations.

In Q2, total comps revenue also increased by only 1%, but Americas declined by an even greater magnitude than in Q1: -4%. However, international comps sales compensated for this with 15% growth. Gross margin moderately increased quarter-over-quarter from 58.3% to 58.5%. Inventories were up even more (21% year-over-year), showing continued demand pressure overall versus growth expectations. Most notably, when including new stores, China's revenue increased by 25% year-over-year, and the rest of the world increase was 19%, with Americas' growth only marginal at 1%.

FY2025 revenue guidance is just 2–4% growth, and there is a $240M reduction to FY2025 gross profit due to tariff headwinds and de minimis removal. In FY2024, the company had a gross profit of $6.27B, which is ~380 bps of gross profit at risk in the near term. Critically, in recent U.S. tariff episodes, tariffs were borne almost entirely by U.S. importers/consumers; this means that pass-through shows up in prices and domestic margins, but there is actually limited foreign supplier absorption—naturally, companies like lululemon suffer (and we are seeing this in the stock price).

lululemon has long been known as a company with immense pricing power, but no pricing power is infinite. Yes, the gross margin is astounding, near 60%, but the -4% America's comps say there's now pushback against management's product/price architecture.

Valuation & Sentiment

From the outset, when looking at the sentiment charts, it's clear that the stock is cheap based on historical sentiment. However, lower sentiment does not mean a good investment, as what is inexpensive relative to its history can often stay so (or worse, lose further value).

That said, even with the stock in a definitive downtrend, I have reason to believe we are near the bottom here. Firstly, consider the surface technicals. The 14-week RSI is 35, which means that we're nearing a long-term oversold condition, which usually results in a rebound occurring of some duration. That said, if macro factors are persistently bad (which in this case could realistically occur given tariffs and China exposure), a 14-week RSI at 30 simply means the worst drawdown is behind us, but not that a positive re-rating is about to occur.

The fundamental valuation factors give us a bit more clarity on what exactly it is we're looking at here. lululemon's forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is -24% lower than the sector median, while the company's forward diluted EPS growth is -79% lower than the sector median. This is not a good sign, as it means that even at this low valuation, from an earnings basis, it is still not attractive relative to the "average," or more precisely stated, the sector median. Forward revenue growth is more positive, at 85% higher than the sector, but the forward price-to-sales ratio is 95% higher than the sector median. From a traditional value investing perspective (which it is wise to adhere to in principle), this stock is not as cheap as it seems when listening to the consensus. But at some point in your investing career, you learn, rightfully, that the best value investors don't listen to the consensus. They're ahead of it.

Given the fundamental valuation indicators, and the unreliability of a successful turnaround with U.S. core growth stifled by tariffs and China exposure, I am not convinced this stock will re-rate in a high-alpha fashion immediately. We could be looking at a similar geopolitical discount here like what Chinese stocks themselves are facing. Likely not the same level of discount, but the principle likely applies to lululemon in the current environment and without the following prerequisites creating a sentiment shift.

Qualitative Analysis

Firstly, lululemon's focus on diversification and U.S. fulfillment shifts (vendor concentration and fabric sourcing), will need to be seen tangibly and not just narrated in order for the market to re-rate LULU's valuation multiples favorably. We can look at this favorably, though; given that the stock price is so low compared to all-time highs, named sourcing or fulfillment changes will lead to positive sentiment before the P&L impact. So, there's a value opportunity here indeed (as long as management is clever enough to execute cleanly from here).

Management clearly needs to narrate China as one spoke in a balanced international wheel that includes China, APAC ex-China, EMEA, Mexico/LatAm, because having China as the sole growth engine is an obvious concentration risk that's heavily intensified by present geopolitics.

Competitively, the end of de minimis could actually help lululemon relative to ultra-cheap competitors that relied on it. Essentially, if lululemon brings more U.S. fulfillment onshore as it protests, the policy shift can blunt price-led competitive encroachment. In this environment, lululemon could shine once again as a "quality" harbor amid low-quality and high-priced goods beginning to saturate. Does the Trump economy favor quality and durability over margins? Maybe.

Management has also admitted that its product life cycles have been running too long, and it's clear that in the future we'll be seeing faster refreshing and clearer price-value architecture, so brand heat can remain strong rather than the company falling into price ploys to maintain consumer interest.

Conclusion: Buy

The drawdown recently for lululemon stock is entirely rational. U.S. comps are negative, inventories are elevated, and tariffs along with the loss of de minimis are cutting ~$240M from gross profit. To compound the sentiment drag, any Western company heavily focused on China to sustain its growth is going to be subject to the "geopolitical discount."

Valuation alone clearly won't unlock upside. Yes, the technicals suggest we may be near a bottom, but we need catalysts for this investment to work. LULU doesn't have an abundance of them, but it has some. We'll need more concrete evidence of supply-chain diversification, U.S. fulfillment shifts, and product refresh to prove this is an opportunity. We can't guarantee that management will execute cleanly, but we can make a concentrated bet that it will. Short-term sentiment can re-rate even before the P&L heals, so I rate LULU a buy at current levels.

data.title: GLD: Put Yield Curve Control On Your Bingo Card
data.description:
The past two decades have been truly unprecedented for anyone who has gotten used to conventional monetary and fiscal policies. To many of us, the concept of "helicopter money" seemed absurd just a decade ago, and yet the response to the pandemic defied these expectations.

What's quite interesting is that we seem to be gradually moving towards a major change of the existing global monetary regime, and as we approach it, the Federal Reserve and the U.S. Treasury seem to embark upon new unconventional policies.

They all seem to work well as far as the immediate consequences on the U.S. consumer, credit, and equity markets are concerned. However, they also seem to gradually erode the trust in the U.S. dollar-based system that we have gotten used to since the fall of the Bretton Woods system in 1971. This process is hardly a surprise to anyone nowadays, and to an extent it is also a logical step. After all, why not exploit the current system if you expect to abandon it or change it soon?

Monetary Cycles

To put all that into perspective, we could use the annual changes of the Conference Board Leading Economic Index® (LEI). First and foremost, we seem to have forgotten what a real recession is, as the last one (outside the COVID pandemic) occurred in the now distant 2007-09 period.

Since then, every time the risk of a recession skyrocketed, i.e., annual change of LEI fell close to or below zero, either the Federal Reserve or the U.S. Treasury (or both) stepped in to provide the necessary stimulus. From quantitative easing and zero-interest rate policy (ZIRP) to tax cuts and helicopter money. No matter how bad things got, the fiscal and monetary authorities were there to support markets.

Based on the LEI graph above, we are once again approaching such a period when the risk of a recession is very high and the Federal Reserve might need to offer more than just the already priced-in rate cuts. Currently, the implied probabilities are for a few rate cuts in the coming months and for the federal funds rate to settle in the range of 3.0% to 3.25% by mid-2026.

Despite the significant risk of inflation, the equity market is already pricing in this more stimulative environment. The cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio (Shiller P/E ratio) has just exceeded 40 - levels that we have seen only once in recorded history.

The yield on 2-year notes has also fallen sharply from above 5% in 2023 to 3.6% as of today. The yield on these notes is a precursor to where the federal funds rate is headed, and with that it is reasonable to assume that the market is already fully pricing in more cuts through the rest of 2025 and early 2026.

Problems That Need To Be Addressed

So far nothing to be particularly worried about, except for the fact that the yield on 10-year Treasuries remains firmly above 4% and has not moved to the extent that rates on the short end of the curve have.

There are a couple of problems associated with these presumably high rates at the long end of the curve. First, there is the issue with interest payments on U.S. debt, which have skyrocketed in recent years.

This is happening at a time when tensions with countries like Russia and China are deteriorating and would most likely continue to do so for the foreseeable future. What this implies is more defense spending on one hand and more government support for strategically important industries like semiconductors, cloud infrastructure, etc.

This suggests that more fiscal spending would be needed in these areas, and this is without even mentioning the looming retirement crisis that Stanley Druckenmiller has been warning about for quite some time now.

Secondly, there's the major issue with house affordability and the deterioration of the U.S. consumer. It's hardly a secret to anyone that the unconventional policies following the pandemic have made housing affordability even worse, and 30-year mortgage rates are now at multi-decade highs. They have come down a bit over the past year, but are nowhere near the rates that borrowers had gotten used to in the period after the Global Financial Crisis.

For these reasons, controlling the long end of the yield curve is becoming increasingly important, but as we all know, the conventional monetary policy tools are oriented towards the short end. Therefore, losing control over long-term rates could spell a disaster.

Moreover, as short-term rates have fallen and the long-term ones have been fairly resistant, we are now witnessing a sustained steepening of the yield curve, which is associated with a higher risk of a recession. More specifically, historical data supports the notion that once the yield curve surpasses its 12-month moving average, then the probability of a recession is quite high.

Moving Towards Yield Curve Control

It seems that many market participants are anticipating that the rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, alongside some populist measures like the tariffs, would be enough to avoid a recession and fill in the widening fiscal gap. By also considering the elevated equity valuations, it appears that long-term rates are also expected to fall and remain low.

But the price of gold does not suggest that. Over the past 2-year period, the price of SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD) has skyrocketed, and in recent months we have seen this trend accelerating.

As I made clear in the past, these upward movements in gold are directly related to the overall risk & uncertainty surrounding the current global monetary regime. And as it happens, the anticipation of more unprecedented measures being implemented in the future drives investors' perceived risk about the system.

For that reason, the recent price action of gold and silver for that matter (see the graph below), confirms that some market participants have positioned themselves for more unprecedented policies being implemented in the coming months.

We should not forget that we have seen yield curve control before - in the years following World War II, from 1942 to 1951. The reason for the yield curve control back then was that the Federal Reserve stepped in to help the U.S. Treasury finance its war debt.

What's quite interesting is that the period of 1942 is very similar to the current one in one very important regard - the level of debt to GDP. As we can see on the graph below, the federal debt held by the public as a share of GDP was above 100% when yield curve control was implemented. The graph below, however, is not updated to reflect the very recent developments in this ratio.

As we can see down below, the ratio skyrocketed during the pandemic and has remained firmly at around 120% since then.

What's causing more uncertainty at present, however, is the risk of inflation. Money supply remains above its long-term trend, and as we can see on the graph below, the spike in velocity of money in recent years resulted in the recent inflationary cycle.

The reason why this is a problem is because back in the 1940s, the Federal Reserve capped long-term rates at a maximum of 2.5%. With yields on 10-year Treasuries now hovering at above 4%, a significant amount of long-term bonds would need to be purchased, thus increasing money supply. Such a scenario risks yet another period of elevated inflation. In the meantime, however, the significant debt levels and interest payments to service that debt are unsustainable.

This conundrum would likely remain a major tailwind for the price of gold and silver. The issue for GLD and iShares Silver Trust (SLV) investors stems from the unprecedented movement in the price of precious metals in 2025, which, in my view, accounts for the short-term risks related to everything said above. That is why I wouldn't be surprised if we experience a notable correction in the coming months, as risks described above are long-term in nature.

Investor Takeaway

Recent increases in the cost of servicing government debt are increasing the likelihood of yield curve control being implemented in the near future. The price of gold seems to reflect that with additional risks stemming from keeping inflation under control while also navigating the need for increased government spending. All that significantly increases the risk of a mistake, exogenous risk even, and even the perceived stability of the global monetary regime. As a result, long-term tailwinds for gold are likely to remain, and the recent price action of the precious metal might be a bit premature as aforementioned risks manifest themselves over long periods of time.

data.title: Humana: Long-Term Recovery Potential, Short-Term Pressure
data.description:
Thesis

Humana Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:HUM) is one of the biggest companies offering Medicare Advantage plans, covering millions of seniors across the U.S. It goes up against large rivals like UnitedHealth (UNH) and CVS Health (CVS) to grow and stay profitable. In its most recent update, Humana said it’s still on track with its 2025 earnings goals and that its 2026 Medicare Advantage Star Ratings came out about the same as last year’s.

We all know that the environment isn’t exactly easy right now. Other companies are also cutting back their Medicare Advantage plans, and overall enrollment in these plans is expected to shrink in 2026.

That puts extra pressure on Humana, especially since its stock has done so poorly, down more than 45% in the past three years, while the S&P 500 (SP500) nearly doubled in the same time.

My research argues that Humana Inc. is at a turning point, perhaps technically (see above), yet certainly fundamentally. Its stock is cheaper compared to its past levels and to competitors, but that lower price already takes into account the pressure it faces from weaker Star Ratings and cuts to Medicare Advantage funding across the industry.

Quality Progress Without Immediate Rewards

Let’s start out with the fact that Medicare Advantage (MA) is getting less money, open enrollment will be tough, and in 2026 Humana, along with other insurers, won’t have any special accounting boosts or extra government payments to make up for tighter funding.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Still, Humana didn’t flinch. Just yesterday, the company’s leaders stuck to their outlook of about $17 in adjusted EPS for 2025 and reported that patient use of care was steady, with hospital admissions and costs per patient not rising faster than expected.

At the big-picture level, the government agency that runs Medicare (CMS) expects Medicare Advantage enrollment to drop from 34.9 million people this year to about 34 million in 2026. That means MA’s share of all Medicare members would shrink from 50% down to around 48%. Large insurance providers have already begun to reduce their offerings, including the number of products they provide and removing themselves from certain counties.

What this really means is that the insurance market is adjusting to less money available and higher healthcare costs. Humana made its adjustments earlier than others by reducing some of its more generous benefits for 2024 and 2025. Additionally, the firm discontinued the provision of certain plans in particular counties, which had an effect on around 550,000 members. That was painful at the time but should be helpful later.
